title: Hermeneutic Profile
description: A structured declaration of the interpretive priors a reader brings to the text. Profiles represent historical traditions or user-defined positions. Every conclusion in the doctrine graph is conditioned on at least one profile.

id.description: A unique, URL-safe identifier for the profile (e.g., 'reformed-westminster', 'catholic-tridentine', 'eastern-orthodox-chalcedonian').

tradition.description: The broad historical tradition this profile represents (e.g., 'Reformed', 'Roman Catholic', 'Eastern Orthodox', 'Lutheran', 'Anabaptist', 'Wesleyan', 'Anglican', 'user-defined').

description.description: A brief prose summary of the profile's distinctive hermeneutical posture, for human readers.

canon.description: Which books are treated as canonical scripture.

tradition.description: The canonical collection recognized by this profile.

notes.description: Any qualifications — e.g., status of deuterocanonicals as 'profitable but not doctrine-establishing' (Anglican Article VI), or specific books included/excluded beyond the named tradition.

text_base.description: Which text editions are treated as primary and secondary witnesses.

ot_primary.description: The primary Old Testament text edition.

nt_primary.description: The primary New Testament text edition.

variant_positions.description: Explicit positions on specific textual variants that matter doctrinally.

reference.description: The passage containing the variant (e.g., '1 John 5:7-8', 'Mark 16:9-20', 'John 7:53-8:11').

variant_name.description: A short identifier for the variant (e.g., 'comma-johanneum', 'longer-ending-mark', 'pericope-adulterae').

position.description: This profile's stance on the variant's authenticity.

notes.description: General notes on text-base approach — e.g., 'LXX preferred where NT authors quote it'.

authority_model.description: How scripture relates to other sources of authority.

notes.description: Qualifications — e.g., how 'tradition' is defined (apostolic oral tradition vs. ecclesiastical tradition), or the role of reason and experience in the Wesleyan model.

interpretive_method.description: The exegetical methods employed, in priority order.

secondary.description: Additional methods employed, in order of weight.

notes.description: How the methods interact — e.g., 'typological readings are grounded in and constrained by the historical-grammatical sense' (Reformed), or 'the spiritual sense never contradicts the literal' (Aquinas).

rule_of_faith.description: Which creeds, confessions, and conciliar definitions function as interpretive guardrails.

confessions.description: Tradition-specific confessional documents that function as binding or normative.

name.description: The confession name (e.g., 'Westminster Confession of Faith', 'Canons and Decrees of Trent', 'Book of Concord', 'Thirty-Nine Articles', 'Schleitheim Confession').

authority_level.description: How much weight this confession carries: binding (must not contradict), normative (strong presumption), instructive (informative but defeasible), historical-reference (context only).

name.description: Council name (e.g., 'Nicaea I', 'Constantinople I', 'Ephesus', 'Chalcedon', 'Constantinople II', 'Constantinople III', 'Nicaea II', 'Trent', 'Vatican I', 'Vatican II').

notes.description: How the rule of faith functions — e.g., 'Scripture is norma normans, confessions are norma normata' (Lutheran), or 'The Magisterium is the authentic interpreter of Scripture' (Catholic).

testament_relation.description: How the Old and New Testaments relate to each other.

position.description: The governing framework for OT-NT relations.

notes.description: Qualifications — e.g., within covenantal theology, whether the covenant of works is affirmed, how the Mosaic covenant relates to the Abrahamic.

inspiration_model.description: The view of how Scripture was inspired and what that entails.

inerrancy.description: If applicable, the inerrancy position. Null if the tradition does not frame the question in these terms.

notes.description: Qualifications — e.g., 'inerrancy applies to autographs, not copies' (Chicago Statement), or 'the Church precedes and authenticates Scripture' (Orthodox).

clarity_hierarchy.description: How apparent tensions between passages are resolved — which texts are treated as controlling for which doctrinal domains.

general_principle.description: The overarching interpretive principle for passage tensions — e.g., 'analogy of faith: clearer passages interpret less clear ones' (Reformed), 'the rule of faith as received in Tradition guides interpretation' (Catholic/Orthodox), 'the New Testament is the interpretive key to the Old' (most Christian traditions).

specific_priorities.description: Explicit priority rules for specific doctrinal tensions. Each rule declares which passage(s) or corpus controls the reading when tension arises.

domain.description: The doctrinal domain where the tension arises (e.g., 'justification', 'perseverance', 'law-and-gospel', 'divine-sovereignty-and-human-agency').

controlling_passages.description: Passages or corpora treated as primary/controlling in this domain.

subordinate_passages.description: Passages or corpora whose reading is shaped by the controlling passages.

rationale.description: Why this priority order holds under this profile's hermeneutic.

version_history.description: Chronological record of modifications to this profile.

commit_sha.description: The git commit SHA where this version was recorded.

author_model.description: The model that authored this version.
